quality.rationale: 基于能动张量守恒的导数转换与高斯定理结合，需多步张量运算及边界条件处理

origin.question: Problem 5.15. Show that in special relativity the stress-energy of an isolated physical system of finite extent obeys the tensor virial theorem$$\int \mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {i j}} \mathrm {d}^{3} \mathbf {x} = \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2}}{\mathrm {d t}^{2}} \int \mathrm {T}^{0 0} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {d}^{3} \mathbf {x}.$$
origin.answer: $\int T^{ij} d^3x = \frac{1}{2} \frac{d^2}{dt^2} \int T^{00} x^i x^j d^3x$
origin.solution: Solution 5.15. The technique here is to use the equations of motion $(\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}, \nu = 0)$ to convert first derivative terms to divergences and then, by Gauss's theorem, to vanishing surface integrals outside the system. By repeated use of the equations of motion we have$$\mathrm {T}_{, 0 0}^{0 0} = - \mathrm {T}_{, \mathrm {k} 0}^{0 \mathrm {k}} = - \mathrm {T}_{, 0 \mathrm {k}}^{\mathrm {k} 0} = \mathrm {T}_{, \mathrm {mk}}^{\mathrm {km}}.$$Now multiply by $\mathbf{x}^i\mathbf{x}^j$$$\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {T}^{0 0}\right)_{, 0 0} = \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} \left(\mathbf {T}^{\mathrm {km}}\right)_{, \mathrm {km}} \\ = \left(\mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {km}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}}\right)_{\mathrm {,km}} - 2 \left(\mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}}\right)_{\mathrm {,k}} \mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {km}}_{\mathrm {,m}} - \mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {km}} \left(\mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}}\right)_{\mathrm {,km}} \\ = \left(\mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {km}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}}\right)_{\mathrm {,km}} - 2 \left(\mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {im}}_{\mathrm {,m}} + \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {jm}}_{\mathrm {,m}}\right) - 2 \mathrm {T}^{\mathrm {ij}}. \\ \end{array}$$The first derivative terms can be simplified, e.g.$$\mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {T}_{, \mathrm {m}}^{\mathrm {im}} = (\mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {T}^{\mathrm {im}})_{, \mathrm {m}} - \delta_{\mathrm {m}}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {T}^{\mathrm {im}} .$$If we make this simplification, integrate over all space, and ignore all divergences which can be converted to vanishing surface integrals we see$$\begin{array}{l} \frac {d^{2}}{d t^{2}} \int x^{i} x^{j} T^{0 0} d^{3} x = \int [ 0 - 2 (- T^{ij} - T^{ij}) - 2 T^{ij} ] d^{3} x \\ = 2 \int T^{ij} d^{3} x. \\ \end{array}$$
